title: Addressables: Runtime Asset Loading, Groups, Labels, Remote Catalogs
summary: Unity's Addressable Asset System (package com.unity.addressables) is the supported successor to the Resources folder and hand-rolled AssetBundles. Assets are flagged addressable and given a string address plus optional labels; at runtime you call Addressables.LoadAssetAsync<T>(key) and receive an AsyncOperationHandle you must Release. Assets are packed into groups with local or remote bundle paths, described by a content catalog that can itself be hosted remotely for live updates without a full player rebuild. Mosaic tools in addressables/* drive this pipeline. The #1 failure mode is forgetting to Release handles (leak refcounts), followed by unassigned AssetReference fields and remote-catalog/player-hash mismatch after an out-of-band content redeploy.

llmGuidance.when_user_wants_runtime_asset_loading_ne: Default to Addressables. Resources/ is legacy and bloats every build; manual AssetBundle APIs are unmaintained. Use addressables/group-create then addressables/entry-add to tag assets.
llmGuidance.when_user_is_in_editor_tooling_only: Do NOT use Addressables. Editor scripts should go through AssetDatabase (kb-core-asset-database). Addressables is a runtime/player system.
llmGuidance.when_user_mentions_live_content_updates_: Point them at remote catalogs. Set the group's BuildPath and LoadPath to the Remote profile variables, enable 'Build Remote Catalog' on AddressableAssetSettings, and host catalog_*.json + .hash + bundles on a CDN. Explain that the player ships with a bootstrap catalog and replaces it at runtime when the remote .hash changes.
llmGuidance.when_debugging_'asset_not_found_at_runti: Check three things in order: (1) the asset is actually marked addressable and its address matches the key used in code (case-sensitive), (2) BuildPlayerContent() was invoked before BuildPipeline.BuildPlayer (stale catalog is the most common cause), (3) for remote, client catalog hash matches what the server is serving.
llmGuidance.when_debugging_'memory_keeps_growing': Every LoadAssetAsync / InstantiateAsync returns an AsyncOperationHandle with a refcount. Every load needs a matching Release (for assets) or ReleaseInstance (for spawned GameObjects). Do not call Release twice — the refcount clamps at 0 but the warning is real and usually means a double-Release bug elsewhere.
llmGuidance.when_user_writes_MonoBehaviour_with_addr: Prefer AssetReference / AssetReferenceT<T> over a raw string key. AssetReference is serializable, drag-and-drop in the Inspector, and LoadAssetAsync() is a member method. String keys silently survive asset renames; AssetReference tracks by GUID.
llmGuidance.when_user_asks_'sync_or_async': Always async. WaitForCompletion() exists on AsyncOperationHandle but it spin-waits on the main thread and can deadlock if the operation needs a frame to progress — only use it for cold-start bootstraps or editor tools, never per-frame gameplay.
llmGuidance.when_building_for_ship: Build content first (AddressableAssetSettings.BuildPlayerContent), THEN player (BuildPipeline.BuildPlayer). Mosaic's addressables/build tool does content-build only; it must run before any player-build tool. Stale content = last-build's bundles shipped with this-build's code.

packageIdentity.name: com.unity.addressables
packageIdentity.manifestHint: Added via Package Manager → Addressables, or manually in Packages/manifest.json. Installing the package drops an Assets/AddressableAssetsData/ folder (AddressableAssetSettings asset, groups, profiles).
packageIdentity.replaces: Resources/ folder auto-include and hand-rolled AssetBundles.Load calls. New projects on Unity 2021.3+ should start here.

coreConcepts.addressableAsset: Any asset with its 'Addressable' checkbox ticked. Gains a string address (defaults to the asset path) and optional labels. The address is the runtime key.
coreConcepts.addressableAssetSettings: Singleton ScriptableObject at Assets/AddressableAssetsData/AddressableAssetSettings.asset. Holds groups, active profile, labels, build scripts. Accessed via AddressableAssetSettingsDefaultObject.Settings.
coreConcepts.groups: A group is a bundling unit. All entries in one group build into AssetBundles sharing a BuildPath/LoadPath pair. Split groups by locality (local vs remote) and churn rate.
coreConcepts.labels: Tag strings on entries. Load many assets in one call: Addressables.LoadAssetsAsync<T>(label, callback). Survive renames; preferable to hand-maintained address lists.
coreConcepts.assetReference: Serializable field type (AssetReference, AssetReferenceT<T>, AssetReferenceSprite, AssetReferenceGameObject). Tracks by GUID + sub-object, drag-target in Inspector, exposes its own LoadAssetAsync / InstantiateAsync.
coreConcepts.contentCatalog: JSON mapping keys + labels → bundle locations + dependencies. Local catalog embedded in StreamingAssets; optional remote catalog on a CDN enables live updates.

signatures: AsyncOperationHandle<GameObject> Addressables.InstantiateAsync(object key, Transform parent = null)
loadingApi.handleLifecycle: Each handle carries a reference count. LoadAssetAsync → 1. A second LoadAssetAsync on the same key → still 1 handle but internal refcount 2. Release decrements; when it hits 0 the bundle is eligible to unload. Keep the handle in a field if you need to Release later — discarding it does not auto-release.
loadingApi.completionSignals: Register handle.Completed += h => { ... } for callback style, or await handle.Task for async/await. handle.Status becomes Succeeded or Failed; check it before dereferencing handle.Result.
loadingApi.waitForCompletion: AsyncOperationHandle.WaitForCompletion() blocks the calling thread until the op finishes. Exists for editor tooling and boot-time critical loads. Avoid in Update/FixedUpdate — it can stall a frame or deadlock against operations that themselves need a frame to progress.

buildPipeline.twoStepRule: Addressables content is NOT built by BuildPipeline.BuildPlayer. You must invoke AddressableAssetSettings.BuildPlayerContent() (or the equivalent menu: Window → Asset Management → Addressables → Groups → Build → New Build → Default Build Script) BEFORE BuildPipeline.BuildPlayer. Skip this and the player ships last build's catalog.
buildPipeline.outputLocations: Local content lands in Library/com.unity.addressables/aa/<platform>/ and is copied into StreamingAssets at player build. Remote content lands in ServerData/<platform>/ by default; ship it to your CDN.
buildPipeline.contentUpdateBuilds: For already-shipped games, use 'Update a Previous Build' to produce a delta catalog + only-changed bundles. Requires the previous build's addressables_content_state.bin. This is how you push DLC or balance patches without a store resubmit.

profiles.purpose: A profile is a named set of path variables (BuildPath, LoadPath, RemoteBuildPath, RemoteLoadPath, etc.). Switching profiles swaps every group's effective paths. Typical set: Default (fully local), Dev (local + localhost remote server), Staging, Production.
profiles.pattern: Per-group dropdown chooses whether BuildPath/LoadPath resolve from Local.* or Remote.* variables. Profile defines those variables. Never hardcode CDN URLs in the group itself — always through a profile variable so you can switch environments from one dropdown.

dependencyTracking.automatic: Addressables walks each marked asset's serialized references and pulls dependent assets (materials, shaders, textures, sub-prefabs) into the build. Shared dependencies across groups get deduped into a shared bundle.
dependencyTracking.implicit_vs_explicit: An asset only loaded transitively (e.g. a material referenced by an addressable prefab) is called an implicit dependency. Its address is auto-generated; you cannot load it by the implicit address reliably across builds. Mark it explicitly addressable if user code needs to load it directly.

decisionRules.scenario: New mobile/console project needs runtime content loading
decisionRules.rule: Use Addressables. Mark gameplay assets addressable, group by churn and locality, ship local groups with the player, optionally host a remote group + remote catalog.
decisionRules.evidence: Resources/ is legacy; Addressables is the actively-developed replacement.

decisionRules.scenario: Writing an editor-only tool that reads project assets
decisionRules.rule: Use AssetDatabase, not Addressables. See kb-core-asset-database. Addressables APIs do not work in edit-mode reliably.
decisionRules.evidence: Addressables build is a player concern; editor tooling uses the raw asset database.

decisionRules.scenario: Team needs DLC or live-balance patches after launch
decisionRules.rule: Put variable content in a Remote group, enable Build Remote Catalog, use 'Update a Previous Build' for deltas, host catalog + bundles on a CDN.
decisionRules.evidence: Content-update is the flagship shipped-title use case.

decisionRules.rule: Resources/ is acceptable. Migrate when runtime asset count grows or remote delivery is planned.
decisionRules.evidence: Addressables overhead is fixed; benefit scales with count and update frequency.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Memory climbs every scene load, never drops
commonPitfalls.cause: AsyncOperationHandles loaded but never Released. Each Load increments a refcount pinning the bundle.
commonPitfalls.fix: Cache the handle, call Addressables.Release(handle) on scene unload or OnDestroy (ReleaseInstance for InstantiateAsync).

commonPitfalls.cause: AssetReference field never assigned in the Inspector; defaults to 'None'.
commonPitfalls.fix: Guard with AssetReference.RuntimeKeyIsValid() before loading, or enforce assignment in OnValidate.

commonPitfalls.symptom: 'Asset not found' or wrong asset after renaming
commonPitfalls.cause: Code uses a string key; asset renamed, address auto-updated, but the string literal did not.
commonPitfalls.fix: Use AssetReference (tracks by GUID) or centralize keys in a single constants file.
Always build content first. addressables/build must run before any player-build step.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Remote catalog clients 'failed to load' after redeploy
commonPitfalls.cause: catalog_<hash>.json filename on the CDN no longer matches the cached hash — partial upload.
commonPitfalls.fix: Deploy catalog .json + .hash + bundles atomically. Validate .hash URL from a test client before rollout.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Warning: 'ResourceManager handle released more times than acquired'
commonPitfalls.cause: Double Release — often OnDisable and OnDestroy both release the same cached handle.
commonPitfalls.fix: Pick one ownership point. Null out the handle after Release so a second call is detectable.

commonPitfalls.symptom: Player size larger than expected; duplicate-inclusion warning
commonPitfalls.cause: Asset is both addressable AND reachable via Resources/ or a direct scene reference; bundled twice.
commonPitfalls.fix: Addressables build log flags duplicates. Remove from Resources/ or inline references; Addressables is the source of truth.

versionDeltas.change: Addressables 1.19.x baseline; stable LoadAssetAsync / Release API, local+remote catalogs, Build Remote Catalog toggle.
Addressables 1.21.x; Content Update workflow refinements, improved build-script API for CI. No public runtime API breaks for LoadAssetAsync family.

Addressables 2.x track; internal build pipeline reworked to share infrastructure with SBP (Scriptable Build Pipeline) improvements. Most user-visible LoadAssetAsync/Release signatures stable; group settings UI restructured.
versionDeltas.migrationNote: Projects upgrading from 1.x to 2.x should re-run content-update state, rebuild catalogs fresh, and verify custom build scripts against the new IBuildScript hooks.

versionDeltas.change: Addressables 2.x maintenance; LTS-track stability. No new public runtime API required for basic load/release flows covered here.

authorshipNotes: Content authored by Mosaic team from publicly-documented Unity Addressables package manual and ScriptReference pages. API signatures, type names, and workflow step names are facts (not copyrightable). All explanatory prose, decision rules, failure-mode diagnoses, and versionDelta commentary are original Mosaic editorial content. Unity's docs are cited as external references and NOT reproduced verbatim. No code samples were copied from Unity's documentation; signatures listed are canonical public API surfaces.
